portfolio: Social Services (note: external reporting suggests NDIS responsibility may have moved to the new Department of Health, Disability and Ageing — confirm current portfolio before engaging)
executive_summary: The NDIA is a large service-delivery agency under intense political and fiscal pressure to make the NDIS financially sustainable while protecting participants. Its 2025-26 Corporate Plan is built around implementing the 'Getting the NDIS Back on Track No.1' Act 2024 (effective 3 October 2024), a new legislative function and key activity targeting fraud and the misuse/abuse of NDIS funds, and the Participant Service Charter/Guarantee with new decision timeframes. New performance measures and revised targets have been introduced under Program 1.2. External signals reinforce a dominant cost-sustainability narrative (an 8% growth target, eligibility reassessments, provider pricing tensions and rising disputes), creating clear, time-bound demand for reform delivery, integrity assurance, financial/actuarial work and participant-facing service redesign. The full PDF was not available for this analysis — opportunities below are grounded in the plan's stated changes plus external research and should be validated against the full document.

strategic_priorities.title: Implementing the 'Getting the NDIS Back on Track' reforms
strategic_priorities.description: Operationalising the wide-ranging legislative changes from the NDIS Amendment Act 2024 that took effect 3 October 2024, which reshape access, planning, budgeting and scheme rules.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: This year's corporate plan reflects changes to NDIS legislation that came into effect on 3 October 2024, through the National Disability Insurance Scheme Amendment (Getting the NDIS Back on Track No.1) Act 2024.

strategic_priorities.title: Fraud and misuse of funds control
strategic_priorities.description: A new legislative function and a new key activity (under Program 1.2) addressing risks related to fraud and the misuse and abuse of NDIS funds.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: We have included an updated legislative function to address risks related to fraud and the misuse and abuse of NDIS funds. A new key activity has been added to the NDIA's performance framework, under Program 1.2.

strategic_priorities.title: Participant Service Guarantee and service improvement
strategic_priorities.description: Delivering against the Participant Service Charter's five engagement principles and new statutory-style timeframes (the Participant Service Guarantee) for access, plan approvals, implementation, plan reviews and nominee changes, plus the practical changes in the Participant Service Improvement Plan.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: It also includes new timeframes, called our Participant Service Guarantee. This sets timeframes for the Agency to make decisions about access, plan approvals, implementation, plan reviews and nominee changes.

strategic_priorities.title: Refreshed performance framework
strategic_priorities.description: New performance measures and updated performance targets accompanying the reform changes, signalling an active reshaping of how the Agency evidences scheme performance.
strategic_priorities.source_quote: And there are new performance measures and updates to performance targets.

reform_and_change_drivers: NDIS Amendment (Getting the NDIS Back on Track No.1) Act 2024 — major legislative reform effective 3 October 2024
New legislative function on fraud and misuse/abuse of NDIS funds
Participant Service Guarantee introducing binding decision timeframes
National Cabinet 8% scheme-growth moderation target and associated eligibility reassessments
Cost-sustainability pressure (spending materially above earlier forecasts)
Provider market instability and recurring Annual Pricing Review tensions
Possible machinery-of-government change moving the NDIS into a new health/disability/ageing department
challenges_and_risks: Fraud, misuse and abuse of NDIS funds — now an explicit legislative function and enterprise concern
Balancing cost moderation (8% target, eligibility reassessments) against participant harm and reputational risk
Rising volume of disputed plan decisions and growing external legal costs
Provider sustainability and pricing adequacy threatening market viability
Meeting new Participant Service Guarantee timeframes at scale across high transaction volumes
Delivering complex legislative reform while maintaining BAU service delivery

news_and_controversies.summary: An AFR opinion piece (26 Sept 2024) framed NDIS cost growth as adding to inflation and rate pressure, drawing pushback from advocates who called the 'economic burden' framing misleading while conceding fraud, red tape and accountability need urgent reform.
news_and_controversies.implication: Cost-sustainability is the dominant political narrative driving efficiency and integrity demand, but advisers must navigate a sensitive 'savings vs participant harm' debate.
news_and_controversies.source: Physical Disability Australia response to AFR — https://www.pda.org.au/2024/09/27/physical-disability-australias-response-to-john-kehoes-afr-article-ndis-cost-blowout-adds-to-inflation-and-rates-pressure-dated-september-26th-september-2024/

news_and_controversies.summary: Reporting indicates NDIS spending well above earlier estimates, alongside a sharp rise in participants disputing plan decisions at the tribunal and growing NDIA spend on external legal firms.
news_and_controversies.implication: Pipeline in actuarial/forecasting, decision-quality and internal-review/dispute-reduction work the Agency is under pressure to fix.
news_and_controversies.source: The Conversation — https://theconversation.com/what-we-know-about-the-ndis-cuts-and-what-theyll-mean-for-people-with-disability-and-their-families-179748 (note: some figures may be dated/superseded)

news_and_controversies.summary: National Cabinet set an 8% annual growth target (scheme still projected near $58bn by 2028); the NDIA has been reassessing participant eligibility, with reports of 28-day evidence deadlines affecting participants including children.
news_and_controversies.implication: Live demand for eligibility-process design, reform implementation and participant communications/assurance; high reputational sensitivity.

news_and_controversies.summary: National Disability Services warned that inadequate NDIA pricing and rising delivery costs pushed quality providers out of the market, framing the next Annual Pricing Review as a possible 'last chance' to prevent market failure (May 2024).
news_and_controversies.implication: Market-design, pricing-review and provider-sustainability advisory needs are pressing and recurrent.

news_and_controversies.summary: The Centre for Independent Studies argued early design flaws and cost overruns flagged by successive actuarial (ASFR) reports were not corrected, and that public reporting on the NDIA's response was lacking.
news_and_controversies.implication: Governance, transparency and assurance-framework work positions a firm around accountability rather than pure cost-cutting.

parliamentary_matters.summary: A National Disability Services media release lodged on ParlInfo criticised the 2024-25 Budget as a 'missed opportunity', flagging NDIS pricing inadequacy and provider exits and pointing to the forthcoming Annual Pricing Review.
parliamentary_matters.implication: Confirms NDIA pricing/reform is an active parliamentary-adjacent issue; useful context but a stakeholder release, not a committee/Estimates/QoN item.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: A new legislative function and new Program 1.2 key activity require the Agency to detect and prevent fraud and misuse/abuse of NDIS funds at scale.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Independent assurance and health check of the new fraud/integrity control framework, with a secondary Data, Analytics & Information stream to design payment-anomaly detection and fraud-analytics capability.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan explicitly adds a legislative function and a new performance-framework key activity on fraud and misuse; external coverage confirms fraud is a top political and integrity pressure, making time-bound assurance and analytics demand credible.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Operationalising sweeping 2024 legislative changes across access, planning and budgeting while maintaining service delivery.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Delivery assurance and embedded program/PMO support for the legislative reform program, including a benefits-management framework and gateway-style assurance at key milestones.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan states the corporate plan reflects the Act effective 3 October 2024 — a named, statutory, multi-year reform that maps cleanly to program delivery and change management at a large service-delivery agency.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Meeting new binding decision timeframes for access, plan approvals, implementation, reviews and nominee changes across very high transaction volumes.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Business/process analysis and service-design discovery on the participant decision pathways underpinning the Guarantee, with process re-engineering and system support to hit timeframes; secondary Programs, Projects & Change for rollout.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan introduces specific Participant Service Guarantee timeframes; meeting them at scale is a process and systems challenge typical of large service-delivery transactional agencies.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: New performance measures and updated targets need to be robust, PGPA-aligned and evidence-based.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A PGPA-aligned performance-measure review and refinement, validating the new Program 1.2 measures and target logic, with a secondary Data, Analytics & Information stream for measurement evidence.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The plan announces new measures and revised targets tied to a new key activity — newly introduced measures frequently need validation and an evidence base.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Moderating scheme growth to the 8% target while spending runs above forecast and disputes/legal costs rise.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: A financial-sustainability review and costing/forecasting support to test cost-driver assumptions and scenario model the impact of reform and eligibility changes.
opportunity_areas.rationale: External research documents the 8% growth target, spending above forecast, and actuarial-warning critiques; the Agency's sustainability mandate creates demand for independent financial analysis. Inferred from external signals rather than the plan landing page itself.

opportunity_areas.agency_need: Communicating eligibility reassessments and reform changes to participants sensitively to reduce harm and reputational risk.
opportunity_areas.suggested_offering: Participant and stakeholder engagement strategy and content support for reassessment and reform communications, aligned to the Participant Service Charter's five engagement principles.
opportunity_areas.rationale: The Charter's engagement principles plus external reporting on contentious 28-day reassessment deadlines indicate a real communications/engagement need; fit is plausible but not named in the plan text.

recommended_actions.action: Request a meeting with the branch owning fraud/integrity (Program 1.2 key activity) and the Chief Risk Officer to discuss independent assurance of the new fraud-control framework and fraud-analytics capability.
recommended_actions.rationale: This is the freshest, most explicit signal in the plan — a new legislative function and key activity — and aligns with the dominant integrity narrative; demand is time-bound and politically backed.

recommended_actions.action: Approach the reform-delivery group leading 'Getting the NDIS Back on Track' implementation with a delivery-assurance and PMO capability statement.
recommended_actions.rationale: A named statutory reform with multi-year implementation is the largest sustained opportunity; positioning early before delivery structures harden is valuable.

recommended_actions.action: Obtain and analyse the full Corporate Plan 2025-26 PDF (6.3MB) to confirm enterprise risks, capability gaps and named initiatives before pitching.
recommended_actions.rationale: This analysis rests on a landing page only; the full plan will sharpen opportunity targeting and confidence levels.

recommended_actions.action: Confirm current portfolio/department ownership and minister(s) for the NDIS following the reported machinery-of-government change.
recommended_actions.rationale: Decision-makers and procurement pathways shift with departmental ownership; pitching to the wrong portfolio wastes effort.

recommended_actions.action: Prepare a short thought piece on meeting binding service-guarantee timeframes through process redesign and decision-quality, targeted at the participant-service group.
recommended_actions.rationale: The Participant Service Guarantee is a concrete operational commitment with clear process/systems implications and links to the rising-disputes problem.

engagement_targets: Branch/division owning the new fraud and misuse-of-funds function (Program 1.2)
Chief Risk Officer and the Audit and Risk Committee
Reform/transformation group leading 'Getting the NDIS Back on Track' implementation
Chief Financial Officer / Scheme Actuary function (sustainability and forecasting)
Group responsible for participant service delivery and the Participant Service Guarantee
Chief Operating Officer / performance and corporate planning function (new performance measures)
talking_points: The new legislative fraud/misuse function and Program 1.2 key activity create an immediate need for independent assurance and fraud-analytics capability — we can both assure the framework and help build the detection capability.
Implementing the 'Back on Track' Act is a multi-year reform program; independent delivery assurance and PMO support reduce the risk of cost and timeframe slippage under political scrutiny.

Meeting the Participant Service Guarantee timeframes at NDIA's transaction volumes is a process-and-systems challenge — service-design and process analysis can lift timeliness while reducing disputes.
New performance measures and targets are an opportunity to lock in a defensible, PGPA-aligned evidence base that withstands Estimates and audit scrutiny.
With spending above forecast and an 8% growth target, independent financial-sustainability and scenario-forecasting work supports the Agency's mandate without framing it purely as cuts.
We are an independent, Commonwealth-focused firm that can advise and build — from assurance through to systems and analytics delivery.
caveats: This analysis is based on the Corporate Plan web landing page only, not the full 6.3MB PDF — so enterprise risks, capability gaps, key-activity detail and performance measures could not be read directly. Several opportunities (financial sustainability, communications) are inferred from external research rather than plan text and are marked accordingly. External research was incomplete: ANAO and Parliament were effectively un-searched due to a tool limit, so those sections are empty and should not be read as 'nothing exists'. Portfolio ownership may have changed (reportedly to a new Health, Disability and Ageing department) and must be confirmed. Recommend obtaining the full PDF and re-running ANAO/Parliament research before acting.
